text: The surgeon performs an ovarian dermoid cystectomy, using a harmonic scalpel to separate the omentum adhered to the cyst. A pedunculated sub-serous fibroid is also removed from the uterus, with the harmonic scalpel carefully separating it from its base.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the fibroid, avoiding the bladder located just behind it. The fibroid is successfully removed and set aside for later morcellation. Attention shifts to the dermoid cyst on the left ovary, with the right ovary appearing normal. The harmonic scalpel is used to dissect the tough dermoid cyst, aiming to maintain a clear dissection plane.

The surgeon uses an endo bag to remove the dermoid cyst, which is held with a grasper. Upon grasping, the cyst ruptures, and suction is immediately applied to remove the fluid. The entire dermoid cyst, along with fatty material, is placed into the endo bag and extracted through a ten mm cannula. After removal, copious lavage with approximately two liters of saline is performed while the patient is in reverse Trendelenburg position to prevent fluid from trickling to the upper abdomen.

The surgeon performs repeated lavage to wash out fluid content, ensuring thorough removal of fatty material from the surgical site. The fibroid is visible and will be removed later. Irrigation is restarted, with fluid poured between the coils of the intestine, using a high flow insufflator during suction.

The left healthy ovary is seen floating, while the remaining spilled content is being suctioned out. Below, the uterus is visible. The surgeon uses an Ethicon Gynecare electrical morcellator to morcellate the entire fibroid, completing the procedure.
